items.q: What is the 'vous' form of 'être' in the present tense?
items.why: Students apply -ez ending from regular verbs. Être is irregular: je suis, tu es, il est, nous sommes, vous ÊTES, ils sont. Memorisation is essential - être appears constantly.

items.q: What is the difference between 'le' and 'lui' as pronouns?
items.correct: Le replaces a direct object (him/it); lui replaces an indirect object (to him/her).
items.random_wrong: Le is masculine, lui is feminine.
items.why: Students don't distinguish object types. 'Je le vois' (I see him - direct); 'Je lui parle' (I speak TO him - indirect). The verb's preposition determines the pronoun type.

items.q: How do you say 'I like coffee' in French?
items.why: English drops articles for general likes/dislikes; French keeps them. For general preferences, use the DEFINITE article: j'aime le chocolat, je déteste les épinards.
It's Aunt. “Uncle” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How does French show female cousin?
items.why: It's Cousine. “Cousin” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many extended family terms in this lesson?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
